title: CLAUDE.md
text: urbnindicators R package provides analysis-ready American Community Survey (ACS) data minimal user effort. main entry point compile_acs_data(), pulls hundreds standardized variables (raw counts + calculated percentages), generates codebook, computes margins error. Five-year ACS estimates ; tract-level geography (block groups) Lifecycle stage: experimental Repository: https://github.com/UI-Research/urbnindicators

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Tests use pre-saved .rds files inst/test-data/ reproducibility speed. test framework testthat edition 3. CI runs GitHub Actions: test-coverage.yaml (push/PR main) pkgdown.yaml (site deployment).
code: # Load package for interactive development devtools::load_all()  # Run all tests devtools::test()  # Run a specific test file testthat::test_file("tests/testthat/test-compile_acs_data.R")  # Check package (full R CMD check) devtools::check()  # Rebuild documentation (after editing roxygen comments) devtools::document()

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Assignment: use = (<-) Indentation: 2 spaces Naming: snake_case functions variables Variable naming pattern: [concept]_[subconcept]_[characteristic]_[metric] (e.g., race_nonhispanic_white_alone_percent) Variable suffixes: _percent percentages, _universe _universe_ universe variables, _M margins error Documentation: roxygen2 (v7.3.2) markdown mode enabled Conditionals: dplyr::if_else() (base ifelse()) Division: use safe_divide(x, y) percentage calculations (returns 0 instead NaN) Iteration/Loops: use functions library(purrr)–avoid loops

title: CLAUDE.md
text: table registry central data structure defines ACS tables package supports. package-level environment (.table_registry) stores table definitions. table definition list containing: name - table identifier (e.g., "snap", "race") description - human-readable description acs_tables - ACS table codes (e.g., "B22003") depends_on - tables table requires (e.g., population_density depends total_population) constructs - (optional) list construct definitions multi-construct tables; name variable_pattern raw_variable_source - raw variables obtained: "manual" (listed explicitly) "select_variables" (resolved runtime via select_variables_by_name()) raw_variables - named vector ACS variable codes (manual sources) compute_fn - function takes .data returns .data derived columns added codebook_entries - structured metadata codebook generation 30+ registered internal tables.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Users can request specific subsets data: Construct-level table names: ACS tables contain multiple constructs. split separate user-facing tables: - sex_by_age (internal) → age + sex (user-facing) - nativity_language (internal) → nativity + language (user-facing) construct names internal names accepted compile_acs_data(tables = ...) resolve_tables(). tables specified: 1. resolve_tables() determines tables needed (always includes total_population) 2. collect_raw_variables() builds named ACS variable vector tables 3. tables’ compute_fn functions called 4. Codebook CVs generated returned variables 5. Tigris geometry fetched spatial = TRUE "population_density" resolved tables
code: # Pull specific tables (using construct-level names) compile_acs_data(tables = c("race", "snap"), years = 2022, geography = "county", states = "NJ")  # Discover available tables and variables list_tables() list_variables()       # tibble of all variables and their table names get_acs_codebook()     # browse ACS variables with clean names and table codes

title: CLAUDE.md
text: R/table_registry.R - Central registry: table definitions, list_tables(), resolve_tables(), collect_raw_variables(), expand_codebook_entry(), register_table() calls. R/list_acs_variables.R - list_acs_variables() (supports optional tables param), select_variables_by_name(), filter_variables(), get_acs_codebook(). R/compile_acs_data.R - compile_acs_data() (tables, deprecated variables), internal_compute_acs_variables() (legacy), safe_divide(). R/generate_codebook.R - generate_codebook() (registry-based) generate_codebook_legacy() (AST-based, deprecated variables path). R/calculate_cvs.R - Computes margins error derived variables (uses standard errors intermediates internally). Parses codebook definition text strings. changes needed adding tables. R/make_pretty_names.R - Converts variable names publication-ready labels. R/utils-pipe.R - Re-exports %>%.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: compile_acs_data(tables, ...) - Pull compute ACS data interpolate_acs(.data, target_geoid, weight, ...) - Aggregate interpolate ACS data custom geographies. weight = NULL complete nesting (direct aggregation); weight = "col" fractional allocation via crosswalk. list_tables() - Available table names tables parameter (construct-level names) get_acs_codebook(year, table) - Browse ACS variables clean names table codes list_variables(year) - Tibble mapping variables (raw + computed) table name list_acs_variables(year, tables) - Named vector ACS variable codes select_variables_by_name(variable_name, census_codebook) - Filter variables pattern filter_variables(variable_vector, match_string, match_type) - filter variables make_pretty_names(.data, .case) - Publication-ready variable names safe_divide(x, y) - Safe division (0 instead NaN)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: add new ACS table package: raw_variables (manual) raw_variable_source (select_variables) raw ACS variables compute_fn calculates derived indicators using safe_divide() dplyr::across() codebook_entries structured entries (types: simple_percent, across_percent, across_sum, complex, one_minus, metadata) Add new global variables utils::globalVariables() call bottom R/table_registry.R Verify: devtools::load_all() list_tables() shows table Verify codebook: codebook auto-generates codebook_entries – changes R/generate_codebook.R needed Verify MOEs: R/calculate_cvs.R parses codebook definition strings – changes needed definitions follow standard patterns Update pretty names needed (R/make_pretty_names.R – rarely needed)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Percentages must 0-1 bounded measures must meaningful, non-missing values least 2 distinct values per measure MOEs reasonable tract-level data Compare published Census Bureau benchmarks available

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Prefer Grep Glob Explore agent code search. codebase small enough targeted searches almost always sufficient far token-efficient. Use parallel Grep/Glob calls multiple patterns needed. Glob: find files name pattern (e.g., **/*registry*.R) Grep: find code content pattern (e.g., right_join.*geometries R/compile_acs_data.R) Read: examine specific file know look use Explore agent genuinely open-ended questions don’t know search (e.g., “authentication system work across whole codebase”). bug fixes, error tracebacks almost always point right file line — start Grep/Read.

title: The urbnindicators Codebook
text: codebook stored attribute dataframe can retrieved attr():

title: The urbnindicators Codebook
text: codebook three columns: Variable: variable name appears dataframe (e.g., snap_received_percent). Type: kind variable. Common types include: count – raw ACS estimate directly API percent – derived ratio, typically count divided universe variable metadata – non-computed variable median geographic identifier Definition: formula describing variable calculated. raw ACS variables, original Census Bureau variable code (e.g., B22003_002). derived variables, expression like snap_received / snap_universe shows numerator denominator. complex definitions may reference multiple raw variables joined + numerator denominator. definition strings also used internally calculate margins error derived variables, accuracy critical.

title: The urbnindicators Codebook
text: Use search box filter variable name, type, definition text. Note codebook reflects variables tables returned list_tables(), specify different tables compile_acs_data() call, codebook comprise different variable listings.

title: Custom Derived Variables
text: Definitions passed compile_acs_data() alongside table names list(). operate columns produced tables request, make sure include table(s) whose variables definitions reference.

title: Custom Derived Variables
text: ’re going create variable describing share white men ages 18 44. needed raw variables ACS table B01001A, described codebook
code: codebook = get_acs_codebook()  codebook %>%   filter(table == "B01001A") %>%   pull(variable_clean) %>%   head() #> [1] "sex_by_age_white_alone_universe"           #> [2] "sex_by_age_white_alone_male"               #> [3] "sex_by_age_white_alone_male_under_5_years" #> [4] "sex_by_age_white_alone_male_5_9_years"     #> [5] "sex_by_age_white_alone_male_10_14_years"   #> [6] "sex_by_age_white_alone_male_15_17_years"

title: Custom Derived Variables
text: Selecting appropriate denominator percentage variables critical, times, complex. basic approach simply divide every variable table universe, contain universe variable name. cases, alternate variables may make insightful denominators. simplicity, just use table universe .

title: Custom Derived Variables
text: express new variable(s) using define_*() helpers pass definitions directly compile_acs_data(). target variable–share white men aged 18-44–requires summing several age-group columns (18-19, 20, 21, 22-24, 25-29, 30-34, 35-39, 40-44) dividing table universe. define_percent() numerator_regex handles : matches columns fitting pattern sums numerator. auto-processed column names B01001A follow pattern sex_by_age_white_alone_male_{age_range}_years, can target 18-44 age groups regex matches leading digits range: definition executed raw ACS data fetched renamed, results appear codebook automatically calculated margins error:
code: df = compile_acs_data(   tables = list(     "B01001A",     define_percent(       "white_male_18_44_percent",       numerator_regex = "sex_by_age_white_alone_male_(1[89]|2[0-9]|3[0-9]|4[0-4])",       numerator_exclude_regex = "percent",       denominator_variables = c("sex_by_age_white_alone_universe"))),   years = 2024,   geography = "county",   states = "NJ")  df %>%   select(GEOID, white_male_18_44_percent) %>%   head() #> # A tibble: 6 × 2 #>   GEOID white_male_18_44_percent #>   <chr>                    <dbl> #> 1 34001                    0.149 #> 2 34003                    0.150 #> 3 34005                    0.162 #> 4 34007                    0.164 #> 5 34009                    0.124 #> 6 34011                    0.160 attr(df, "codebook") %>%   filter(calculated_variable == "white_male_18_44_percent") %>%   pull(definition) #> [1] "Numerator = sex_by_age_white_alone_male_18_19_years (B01001A_007), sex_by_age_white_alone_male_20_24_years (B01001A_008), sex_by_age_white_alone_male_25_29_years (B01001A_009), sex_by_age_white_alone_male_30_34_years (B01001A_010), sex_by_age_white_alone_male_35_44_years (B01001A_011). Denominator = sex_by_age_white_alone_universe (B01001A_001)."

title: Custom Derived Variables
text: One numerator, one denominator:
code: df_snap = compile_acs_data(   tables = list(     "snap",     define_percent(       "snap_received_percent_manual",       numerator = "snap_received",       denominator = "snap_universe")),   years = 2024,   geography = "county",   states = "NJ")  ## verify it matches the built-in version df_snap %>%   transmute(     builtin = snap_received_percent,     manual = snap_received_percent_manual,     match = abs(builtin - manual) < 1e-10) %>%   filter(!match) #> # A tibble: 0 × 3 #> # ℹ 3 variables: builtin <dbl>, manual <dbl>, match <lgl>

title: Custom Derived Variables
text: Percentages race categories, plus person--color complement. built-race table works internally.
code: compile_acs_data(   tables = list(     "race",     define_across_percent(       input_regex = "^race_nonhispanic|^race_hispanic",       output_suffix = "_percent",       denominator = "race_universe"),     ## race_personofcolor_percent is the share of all individuals who are not     ## non-Hispanic, White alone, i.e., the complement     define_one_minus(       "race_personofcolor_percent",       source_variable = "race_nonhispanic_white_alone_percent")),   years = 2024,   geography = "county",   states = "DC")

title: Custom Derived Variables
text: Sum male + female counts combined age variables, calculate percentages. built-sex_by_age table works internally. one bit tricky–source table includes variables age group, split sex. get age groups, add two sex-specific estimates given age group. requires us specify input_regex, selects, case, female-specific age variables. addend_function programmatically identifies -named, male-specific variables. output_naming_function simplifies resulting combined variable, removing sex category extraneous words.
code: compile_acs_data(   tables = list(     "sex_by_age",     define_across_sum(       input_regex = "sex_by_age_female_.*years($|_over$)",       addend_function = function(column) {         column %>% stringr::str_replace("female", "male")},       output_naming_function = function(column) {         column %>% stringr::str_replace("sex_by_age_female_", "age_")       }),     define_across_percent(       input_regex = "^age.*years($|_over$)",       output_suffix = "_percent",       denominator = "sex_by_age_universe")),   years = 2024,   geography = "county",   states = "DC")

title: Custom Derived Variables
text: cases numerator denominator requires summing subtracting multiple variables, use numerator_variables, denominator_variables, optional *_subtract_* arguments:

Note simple case interpolation every source geography maps one one target geography. address complex case later.
code: dc_tracts = compile_acs_data(   years = 2024,   tables = "snap",   geography = "tract",   states = "DC",   spatial = TRUE)

title: Translating ACS Data to Custom Geographies
text: illustration, ’ll create four quadrants DC assigning tracts quadrant based centroid coordinates. Next, dissolve tract geometries quadrant boundaries. calling interpolate_acs() function drops geometry output. Now aggregate data interpolate_acs(). weight = NULL (default), sums count variables, recalculates percentages summed components, propagates margins error. Finally, rejoin dissolved geometry aggregated data mapping.
code: # Calculate tract centroids and assign to quadrants dc_tracts = dc_tracts %>%   mutate(     centroid = st_centroid(geometry),     longitude = st_coordinates(centroid)[, 1],     latitude = st_coordinates(centroid)[, 2],     quadrant = case_when(       longitude < median(longitude) & latitude >= median(latitude) ~ "Northwest",       longitude >= median(longitude) & latitude >= median(latitude) ~ "Northeast",       longitude < median(longitude) & latitude < median(latitude) ~ "Southwest",       longitude >= median(longitude) & latitude < median(latitude) ~ "Southeast")) %>%   select(-centroid, -longitude, -latitude) # Dissolve tract boundaries into quadrant polygons quadrant_geometry = dc_tracts %>%   group_by(quadrant) %>%   summarise(geometry = st_union(geometry), .groups = "drop") # Aggregate to quadrants dc_quadrants = interpolate_acs(   .data = dc_tracts,   target_geoid = "quadrant") # Rejoin quadrant geometry for mapping dc_quadrants_sf = quadrant_geometry %>%   left_join(dc_quadrants, by = c("quadrant" = "GEOID"))

code: # Calculate DC-wide SNAP rate for comparison dc_snap_rate = sum(dc_tracts$snap_received, na.rm = TRUE) /                sum(dc_tracts$snap_universe, na.rm = TRUE)  # Test significance at tract level tracts_sig = dc_tracts %>%   mutate(     significant = tidycensus::significance(       est1 = snap_received_percent,       est2 = dc_snap_rate,       moe1 = snap_received_percent_M,       moe2 = 0.005,       clevel = 0.9),     difference = case_when(       !significant ~ "Not significant",       snap_received_percent > dc_snap_rate ~ "Higher than DC average",       snap_received_percent < dc_snap_rate ~ "Lower than DC average"))  # Test significance at quadrant level quadrants_sig = dc_quadrants_sf %>%   mutate(     significant = tidycensus::significance(       est1 = snap_received_percent,       est2 = dc_snap_rate,       moe1 = snap_received_percent_M,       moe2 = 0.005,       clevel = 0.9),     difference = case_when(       !significant ~ "Not significant",       snap_received_percent > dc_snap_rate ~ "Higher than DC average",       snap_received_percent < dc_snap_rate ~ "Lower than DC average"))  # Color palette sig_colors = c(   "Higher than DC average" = "#1696D2",   "Lower than DC average" = "#FDBF11",   "Not significant" = "#D2D2D2")  # Maps map_tracts_sig = tracts_sig %>%   ggplot() +   geom_sf(aes(fill = difference), color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +   scale_fill_manual(values = sig_colors, na.value = "grey80") +   urbnthemes::theme_urbn_map() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(fill = "", subtitle = "Tract-level", title = "")  map_quadrants_sig = quadrants_sig %>%   ggplot() +   geom_sf(aes(fill = difference), color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +   scale_fill_manual(values = sig_colors, na.value = "grey80") +   urbnthemes::theme_urbn_map() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(fill = "", subtitle = "Quadrant-level", title = "")  gridExtra::grid.arrange(   map_tracts_sig, map_quadrants_sig,   ncol = 2,   top = grid::textGrob(     "Aggregation can mitigate challenges with small-population, high-error observations" %>% str_wrap(60),     gp = grid::gpar(fontsize = 12, fontface = "bold")))

Providing weight column enables proportional allocation partial-overlap crosswalks. Count variables MOEs multiplied crosswalk weight summing; percentages recalculated allocated components.
code: # renv::install("UI-Research/crosswalk")  # Pull 2019 ACS data (2010-vintage tracts) and 2024 ACS data (2020-vintage tracts) dc_tracts = compile_acs_data(   years = c(2019, 2024),   tables = "snap",   geography = "tract",   states = "DC",   spatial = TRUE)  # Get 2010→2020 tract crosswalk (population-weighted) tract_crosswalk = crosswalk::get_crosswalk(   source_geography = "tract",   target_geography = "tract",   source_year = 2010,   target_year = 2020,   weight = "population")  # Extract the crosswalk tibble and rename columns for interpolate_acs() tract_xwalk = tract_crosswalk$crosswalks$step_1 %>%   filter(weighting_factor == "weight_population") %>%   select(     GEOID = source_geoid,     target_tract = target_geoid,     weight = allocation_factor_source_to_target)  # Interpolate 2019 data to 2020-vintage tract boundaries dc_2019_in_2020_tracts = interpolate_acs(   .data = dc_tracts %>% filter(data_source_year == 2019) %>% st_drop_geometry(),   target_geoid = "target_tract",   weight = "weight",   crosswalk = tract_xwalk)  # Join crosswalked 2019 estimates to 2024 sf data and calculate change. # The 2019 (2015-2019) and 2024 (2020-2024) 5-year ACS don't overlap, # so estimates are independent and MOE_diff = sqrt(MOE_1^2 + MOE_2^2). snap_change = dc_tracts %>%   filter(data_source_year == 2024) %>%   left_join(     dc_2019_in_2020_tracts %>%       select(GEOID,              snap_received_percent_2019 = snap_received_percent,              snap_received_percent_2019_M = snap_received_percent_M),     by = "GEOID") %>%   mutate(     snap_ppt_change = (snap_received_percent - snap_received_percent_2019) * 100,     snap_ppt_change_M = sqrt(snap_received_percent_M^2 + snap_received_percent_2019_M^2) * 100,     significant = abs(snap_ppt_change) > snap_ppt_change_M * 1.645 / 1.645,     change_category = case_when(       !significant ~ "Not significant",       snap_ppt_change > 0 ~ "Significant increase",       snap_ppt_change < 0 ~ "Significant decrease"))  # Map 1: percentage-point change in SNAP receipt map_change = ggplot(snap_change) +   geom_sf(aes(fill = snap_ppt_change), color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +   scale_fill_gradient2(     low = "#FDBF11",     mid = "#D2D2D2",     high = "#1696D2",     midpoint = 0) +   theme_urbn_map() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   guides(fill = guide_colorbar(direction = "horizontal", title.position = "top")) +   labs(fill = "Change (ppt)", subtitle = "Percentage-point change")  # Map 2: statistical significance of the change change_colors = c(   "Significant increase" = "#1696D2",   "Significant decrease" = "#FDBF11",   "Not significant" = "#D2D2D2")  map_sig = ggplot(snap_change) +   geom_sf(aes(fill = change_category), color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +   scale_fill_manual(values = change_colors, na.value = "grey80") +   theme_urbn_map() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   guides(fill = guide_legend(direction = "horizontal", title.position = "top")) +   labs(fill = "", subtitle = "Statistical significance (relative to zero change)")  gridExtra::grid.arrange(   map_change, map_sig,   ncol = 2,   top = grid::textGrob(     "Change in SNAP receipt, 2019 to 2024",     gp = grid::gpar(fontsize = 12, fontface = "bold")))

Variables codebook original API names included definitions can cross-reference needed. Use consistent variable naming convention. Variable names follow pattern [concept]_[subconcept]_[characteristic]_[metric]. example, race_nonhispanic_white_alone_percent. _percent suffix always denotes derived percentage, _universe denotes denominator used calculate percentages table, _M denotes margin error. consistency makes easy use dplyr::matches() dplyr::starts_with() select related groups variables. Use scales::percent() display formatting. can always just multiply values (MOEs) 100 prefer; multiplication requires adjustments MOEs. code: acs_df_county = compile_acs_data(   years = c(2024),   tables = "age",   geography = "county",   states = "NJ")  acs_df_tract = compile_acs_data(   years = c(2024),   tables = "age",   geography = "tract",   states = "NJ",   spatial = TRUE) ## derive CVs from MOEs for quality evaluation plot_df = bind_rows(       acs_df_county %>% mutate(geography = "County"),       acs_df_tract %>% mutate(geography = "Tract")) %>%     st_drop_geometry() %>%     select(       geography,       c(matches("^age.*percent$") & matches("(_6|_7|_8)")),       c(matches("^age.*percent_M$") & matches("(_6|_7|_8)"))) %>%     pivot_longer(-geography) %>%     mutate(       base_var = str_remove(name, "_M$"),       type = if_else(str_detect(name, "_M$"), "moe", "estimate")) %>%     pivot_wider(       id_cols = c(geography, base_var),       names_from = type,       values_from = value,       values_fn = list) %>%     tidyr::unnest(c(estimate, moe)) %>%     mutate(       cv = (moe / 1.645) / estimate * 100,       cv = if_else(is.infinite(cv), NA_real_, cv),       name = base_var %>%         str_replace_all(c("_" = " ", "percent" = "(%)")) %>%         str_remove_all("age") %>%         str_squish() %>% str_trim(),       plot_title = str_c(geography, ": ", name))  factor_levels = plot_df %>%   arrange(name) %>%   distinct(plot_title, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%   pull(plot_title)  plot_df %>%   mutate(     plot_title = factor(plot_title, levels = factor_levels, ordered = TRUE)) %>%   ggplot() +     geom_histogram(       aes(x = cv), fill = palette_urbn_main[1], bins = 50) +     geom_vline(xintercept = 30, linetype = "dashed") +     facet_wrap(~ plot_title, ncol = 2, scales = "free") +     scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 150)) +     guides(fill =  "none") +     theme_urbn_print() +     theme(axis.text.y = element_blank()) +     labs(       x = "CV (derived from MOE)",       y = "Distribution",       title = "County CVs are Smaller than Tract CVs\nCVs for Wider Age Ranges are Smaller than CVs for Narrow Age Ranges",       subtitle = "Coefficients of variation at the county and tract levels for various age groupings")

title: urbnindicators
text: ’s lot happening behind scenes, ’s important understand variable represents calculated. library(urbnindicators) includes codebook attribute dataframe returned compile_acs_data(). View navigate full codebook . Access codebook attr(): codebook three columns: calculated_variable – variable name appears dataframe. variable_type – whether variable count (raw ACS estimate), percent (derived ratio), metadata (e.g., median geographic identifier). definition – formula showing variable calculated. raw ACS variables, original Census Bureau variable code (e.g., B22003_002). derived variables, expression like snap_received / snap_universe. definition strings also used internally calculate margins error derived variables (see Quantifying Survey Error), accuracy critical. definitions quite complex. example, disability_percent sum sex--age groupings people disabilities (numerator) divided table universe:
code: codebook = attr(df_urbnindicators, "codebook")  codebook %>%   head(5) #> # A tibble: 5 × 9 #>   calculated_variable                    variable_type definition numerator_vars #>   <chr>                                  <chr>         <chr>      <list>         #> 1 total_population_universe              Count         This is a… <chr [0]>      #> 2 means_transportation_work_universe     Count         This is a… <chr [0]>      #> 3 means_transportation_work_car_truck_v… Count         This is a… <chr [0]>      #> 4 means_transportation_work_car_truck_v… Count         This is a… <chr [0]>      #> 5 means_transportation_work_car_truck_v… Count         This is a… <chr [0]>      #> # ℹ 5 more variables: numerator_subtract_vars <list>, denominator_vars <list>, #> #   denominator_subtract_vars <list>, se_calculation_type <chr>, #> #   aggregation_strategy <chr> codebook %>%   filter(calculated_variable == "disability_percent") %>%   pull(definition) #> [1] "Numerator = sex_by_age_by_disability_status_male_under_5_years_with_a_disability (B18101_004), sex_by_age_by_disability_status_male_5_17_years_with_a_disability (B18101_007), sex_by_age_by_disability_status_male_18_34_years_with_a_disability (B18101_010), sex_by_age_by_disability_status_male_35_64_years_with_a_disability (B18101_013), sex_by_age_by_disability_status_male_65_74_years_with_a_disability (B18101_016), sex_by_age_by_disability_status_male_75_years_over_with_a_disability (B18101_019), sex_by_age_by_disability_status_female_under_5_years_with_a_disability (B18101_023), sex_by_age_by_disability_status_female_5_17_years_with_a_disability (B18101_026), sex_by_age_by_disability_status_female_18_34_years_with_a_disability (B18101_029), sex_by_age_by_disability_status_female_35_64_years_with_a_disability (B18101_032), sex_by_age_by_disability_status_female_65_74_years_with_a_disability (B18101_035), sex_by_age_by_disability_status_female_75_years_over_with_a_disability (B18101_038). Denominator = sex_by_age_by_disability_status_universe (B18101_001)."

title: Out of the Box Social Science Indicators from the American Community Survey (ACS)
text: compile_acs_data() makes easy pull multiple years produce publication-ready visualizations:
code: plot_data = df %>%   transmute(     county_name = NAME %>% str_remove(" County, New Jersey"),     race_personofcolor_percent,     race_personofcolor_percent_M,     data_source_year = factor(data_source_year))  state_averages = plot_data %>%   summarize(     .by = data_source_year,     mean_pct = mean(race_personofcolor_percent)) %>%   arrange(data_source_year) %>%   pull(mean_pct)  ## order counties by 2019 value for the dumbbell plot county_order = plot_data %>%   filter(data_source_year == "2019") %>%   arrange(race_personofcolor_percent) %>%   pull(county_name)  plot_data = plot_data %>%   mutate(county_name = factor(county_name, levels = county_order))  dumbbell_data = plot_data %>%   pivot_wider(     id_cols = county_name,     names_from = data_source_year,     values_from = race_personofcolor_percent,     names_prefix = "year_")  ggplot() +   geom_segment(     data = dumbbell_data,     aes(       x = county_name,       y = year_2019,       yend = year_2024),     color = palette_urbn_main[7],     linewidth = 1) +   ggdist::stat_gradientinterval(     data = plot_data,     aes(       x = county_name,       ydist = distributional::dist_normal(         race_personofcolor_percent,         race_personofcolor_percent_M / 1.645),       color = data_source_year),     point_size = 2,     .width = .95) +   geom_hline(     yintercept = state_averages[1],     linetype = "dashed",     color = palette_urbn_main[1]) +   geom_hline(     yintercept = state_averages[2],     linetype = "dashed",     color = palette_urbn_main[2]) +   annotate(     "text",     y = state_averages[1] - .15,     x = 21.5,     label = "State mean (2019)",     fontface = "bold.italic",     color = palette_urbn_main[1],     size = 9 / .pt,     hjust = 0,     nudge_y = .01) +   annotate(     "text",     y = state_averages[2] + .01,     x = 21.5,     label = "State mean (2024)",     fontface = "bold.italic",     color = palette_urbn_main[2],     size = 9 / .pt,     hjust = 0,     nudge_y = .01) +   labs(     title = "All NJ Counties Experienced Racial Diversification from 2019 to 2024",     subtitle = paste0("Share of population who are people of color, by county, 2019-2024 Confidence intervals are presented around each point but are extremely small"),     x = "",     y = "Share of population who are people of color") +   scale_x_discrete(expand = expansion(mult = c(.03, .04))) +   scale_y_continuous(     breaks = c(0, .25, .50, .75, 1.0),     limits = c(0, .75),     labels = scales::percent) +   coord_flip() +   theme_urbn_print()

title: Out of the Box Social Science Indicators from the American Community Survey (ACS)
text: ACS data available standard geographies (tracts, counties, states, etc.), many analyses require non-standard areas like neighborhoods, school zones, planning districts. interpolate_acs() aggregates source data user-defined geography, properly re-deriving percentages propagating margins error: See vignette("custom-geographies") .
code: dc_tracts = compile_acs_data(   tables = "snap",   years = 2024,   geography = "tract",   states = "DC",   spatial = TRUE)  ## assign each tract to a quadrant based on its centroid dc_tracts = dc_tracts %>%   mutate(     centroid = sf::st_centroid(geometry),     lon = sf::st_coordinates(centroid)[, 1],     lat = sf::st_coordinates(centroid)[, 2],     quadrant = case_when(       lon <  median(lon) & lat >= median(lat) ~ "NW",       lon >= median(lon) & lat >= median(lat) ~ "NE",       lon <  median(lon) & lat <  median(lat) ~ "SW",       lon >= median(lon) & lat <  median(lat) ~ "SE")) %>%   select(-centroid, -lon, -lat)  ## aggregate tracts to quadrants dc_quadrants = interpolate_acs(   .data = dc_tracts,   target_geoid = "quadrant")  dc_quadrants %>%   sf::st_drop_geometry() %>%   select(GEOID, snap_received_percent, snap_received_percent_M) #>   GEOID snap_received_percent snap_received_percent_M #> 1    NE            0.15951925             0.019448994 #> 2    NW            0.07036185             0.006889427 #> 3    SE            0.24445974             0.012073306 #> 4    SW            0.06525691             0.012003668

what: Aggregate or interpolate ACS data to custom geographies — interpolate_acs
title: Aggregate or interpolate ACS data to custom geographies — interpolate_acs
text: Aggregate interpolate ACS data source geographies user-defined target geographies. Supports two modes: Complete nesting (weight = NULL): source geography maps entirely one target geography. Count variables summed, percentages recalculated summed components, intensive variables (medians, averages) computed population-weighted averages. Fractional allocation (weight = "column_name"): Source geographies can split across multiple targets using crosswalk weights. Count variables MOEs multiplied weight summing. Percentages recalculated interpolated components. Intensive variables use allocated population weights. MOE propagation uses Census Bureau approximation formulas throughout. Crosswalk weights treated constants (sampling error).

title: Aggregate or interpolate ACS data to custom geographies — interpolate_acs
code: interpolate_acs(   .data,   target_geoid,   weight = NULL,   crosswalk = NULL,   source_geoid = "GEOID",   weight_variable = "total_population_universe" )

title: Aggregate or interpolate ACS data to custom geographies — interpolate_acs
text: .data dataframe returned compile_acs_data(). Must codebook attribute attached. target_geoid Character. Column name target geography identifiers. Must exist .data crosswalk. result renames column GEOID. weight Character NULL. NULL (default), assumes complete nesting source geography maps entirely one target. column name provided, performs fractional allocation using column weights. Weights sum approximately 1 per source geography. crosswalk data frame containing crosswalk mapping. Optional modes. provided, joined .data via source_geoid processing. Must include columns source_geoid target_geoid (weight fractional allocation used). source_geoid Character. Column name source geography identifiers. Must exist .data (crosswalk provided). Default "GEOID". weight_variable Character. Variable name used population-weighted averages intensive variables (medians, averages, etc.). Default "total_population_universe".

title: Aggregate or interpolate ACS data to custom geographies — interpolate_acs
text: dataframe aggregated target geographies recalculated estimates, MOEs, percentages. modified codebook attached attribute.

title: Aggregate or interpolate ACS data to custom geographies — interpolate_acs
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # First, create tract-level data tract_data = compile_acs_data(   tables = c("race", "snap"),   years = 2022,   geography = "tract",   states = "DC" )  # Complete nesting: each tract belongs to exactly one neighborhood tract_data$neighborhood = c("Downtown", "Downtown", "Uptown", ...) neighborhood_data = interpolate_acs(   .data = tract_data,   target_geoid = "neighborhood" )  # Fractional allocation with a crosswalk crosswalk = data.frame(   GEOID = c("11001000100", "11001000100", "11001000201"),   neighborhood = c("Downtown", "Chinatown", "Downtown"),   alloc_weight = c(0.6, 0.4, 1.0) )  neighborhood_data = interpolate_acs(   .data = tract_data,   target_geoid = "neighborhood",   weight = "alloc_weight",   crosswalk = crosswalk ) } # }
